introSection: Timber Terror is a wooden roller coaster located at Silverwood Theme Park in Athol, Idaho, USA.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Originally opened in 1996, the ride had been called Grizzly, but the park had to change the name to Timber Terror in 1997 to avoid litigation and confusion with the wooden roller coasters of the same name at Paramount's Great America and Paramount's Kings Dominion. Rocky Mountain Construction founder, Fred Grubb, assisted with the initial construction of the ride.

The ride was designed by park owner and founder, Gary Norton. Similar to the park's larger wooden coaster Tremors, the design was finalized by Custom Coasters International, Inc. and the ride was constructed in house.

On September 26, 2010, Timber Terror soft opened to the public with a reversed train as part of the park's annual Scarywood Haunted Nights event. The train received new headrests as part of the transformation. It has run in reverse for Scarywood and the last weekend in September since.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: Timber Terror has a height of, a drop of and a max speed of.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The trains are manufactured by Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters.

fullText:
Timber Terror is a wooden roller coaster located at Silverwood Theme Park in Athol, Idaho, USA.

Originally opened in 1996, the ride had been called Grizzly, but the park had to change the name to Timber Terror in 1997 to avoid litigation and confusion with the wooden roller coasters of the same name at Paramount's Great America and Paramount's Kings Dominion. Rocky Mountain Construction founder, Fred Grubb, assisted with the initial construction of the ride.

The ride was designed by park owner and founder, Gary Norton. Similar to the park's larger wooden coaster Tremors, the design was finalized by Custom Coasters International, Inc. and the ride was constructed in house.

On September 26, 2010, Timber Terror soft opened to the public with a reversed train as part of the park's annual Scarywood Haunted Nights event. The train received new headrests as part of the transformation. It has run in reverse for Scarywood and the last weekend in September since.

Timber Terror has a height of, a drop of and a max speed of.

The trains are manufactured by Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters.
